data.title: Midstream Goldmine: Targa Resources Taps The Permian Boom
data.description:
Overview

Targa Resources (NYSE:TRGP) is a midstream energy company which is focused on gathering, processing, transporting and marketing natural gas and natural gas liquids. It is based primarily in the Permian basin and other shale plays in USA. It has two primary segments – gathering & processing (G&P) and logistics & transportation (L&T). This indicates that Targa Resources has end-to-end production facilities for its sites. The G&P segment is responsible for the gathering of the raw gas from the wells, removing impurities and extracting the natural gas liquids. The L&T segment is responsible for fractional distillation into different products and operating pipelines, storage and export terminals which helps in moving and marketing of the NGLs and LPGs overseas. A point to be noted about Targa Resources is that the pricing model is mostly fee-based which partially hedges it against the commodity price volatility.

The recent earnings call for Q1 FY25 revealed that Targa Resources had a record EBITDA of $1.179 billion which represents a growth of 22% and this is driven by 90% fee-based business which strengthens the case that this is a sustainable growth and not caused by rise in commodity prices. In the call it was also mentioned that they will also be focusing on dividend growth and share repurchases. Targa has an asset footprint across the Permian, Eagle Ford, Barnett, etc.

Targa is focusing heavily on capital expenditure (as TTM capex is more than $3 billion) and it is 82.84% of operating cash flows and there has been a growth of 24.33% from FY23 to FY24. Based on the reports from the recent earnings call and analysis of the financials, the result of the investments have also paid off. Since FY15 to FY24, the net income has grown at a CAGR of 41.34%.

Tailwinds

NGL Demand & Permian Growth

The natural gas market is likely to experience significant growth mainly from the markets of North America and the Middle East. The figure below highlights the geography-wise breakdown of the demand for NGLs. It is expected to witness a global CAGR of 6% YoY. This presents significant growth opportunities for the overall industry.

Moreover, the North American market is expected to display significant growth as well driven by increased output and demand. Moreover, an additional factor to keep in mind is that Targa Resources is an upstream firm and therefore is likely to witness a positive bullwhip impact from the tailwind in the NGL market. In one of my previous articles on SM Energy (SM) I have also mentioned what are the forces currently facing the oil & gas market as well and one of them is the political support that the current US administration is providing.

In addition to these factors, is the tailwind that the Permian basin production is increasing. The Permian basin is one of the most prolific and largest oil producing regions in the world and is located in West Texas and southeastern Mexico. It covers a total area of 75,000 square miles and produces 5.6 million barrels of oil per day (as of FY24). It has sub-basins – Midland basin, Delaware basin, Central basin platform. The Permian basin in itself accounts for 40% of US crude oil output and 20% of US natural gas output. The figure below displays the trend in Permian oil production.

The figure highlights that Permian oil production is only expected to increase based on the past patterns. This has been driving multiple natural gas explorers to step up oil & natural gas exploration in the Permian basin. Therefore, the positive expectation is also acting as a positive tailwind for the business of Targa Resources as well. Note that there are pipeline systems already present where Targa has ownership and the growth in the Permian basin will add to their revenues since they have already incurred the expenses. Therefore, since the key infrastructure is already present for Targa, increased volume of exploration will lead to higher revenues through volume-based fees. Moreover, given the high set-up costs associated with the infrastructure in which Targa Resources is operating, the market acts very similar to a natural monopoly. Therefore, increased volume that Targa handles helps in reducing the average costs per volume of operation.

However, Targa is also adding to their own capacity by building newer infrastructure to support the rising demand and also add to their own supply capacity. Moreover, Targa has actively taken steps to maintain financial discipline. The figure below highlights the same in terms of the liquidity ratios and debt-to-equity ratio.

The figure above shows that Targa has maintained their liquidity ratios but except for the dip in 2018 which highlights their aggressive investment strategy. However, a look at the debt-to-equity ratio illustrates that the Targa management has been actively taking steps to implement steps to deleverage its balance sheet which stands out clearly in favour of the shareholders.

Fee Based Pricing

One of the biggest sources of risks for energy companies like Targa Resources is the exposure to commodity price volatility which leads to fluctuations in earnings that numerous long-term value investors are averse to. Moreover, this also drives the management away from long-term investment plans due to uncertainty regarding the cash flows as well. However, Targa Resources has managed to counter this risk effectively by implementing the fee-based pricing model and that has ensured greater stability in earnings every year. Based on the recent earnings call of Q1 FY25, Targa Resources has managed to ensure that 90% of the revenues come from the fee-based pricing model. The figure below shows the breakdown of the pricing model. With such a heavy proportion of revenues coming from a more stable source, a key risk factor is effectively eliminated.

Share Repurchase & Dividend Payout

One of the announcements made by the Targa Resources management in the Q1 FY25 earnings call is the share repurchase programme and the dividend payout policy. This is likely to stand them in good stead with the value investors. The share repurchase programme is likely to indicate undervaluation currently of the shares which even the management is likely to take advantage of by cashing in during this time. In the call, the management announced a 33% increase in dividend payout in Q1 of FY25 relative to Q1 of FY24.

Valuation & Financials

In order to understand which best method to follow for valuing the Targa, we need to understand the nature of the earnings and cash flows. Most of Targa’s earnings come from fee-based pricing which indicates relatively stable cash flow. However, there is also significant capex that the company has been undertaking. Therefore, we can follow a relative ratio-based valuation. However, to understand the intrinsic value, we can apply DCF valuation by projecting the future cash flows given that the cash inflows are relatively stable due to the pricing model. However, to estimate accurately, we have to undertake a three-phased valuation approach given that currently Targa is growing rapidly. The figure below provides the valuation as well.

Since in the past years the ratio between operating cash flow and operating income were relatively stable we have taken a moving average ratio. I expect the capex spendings to continue and grow at a 5% rate (previously it had much higher rating but it slows down gradually). I expect the revenue growth rate to continue as it is at around 10%. However, it will gradually level out to 3% growth for the remaining period. That is why I have assumed an interim 5 year period when the growth rate will be 6%. The outcome is that the intrinsic per share value comes to around $197 which gives an upside of nearly 21%, setting a justification to make it a buy case.

Risks

Heavy Dependence on Permian Basin

Not so surprisingly, one of the key strengths of Targa Resources is also its weakness because it reveals its vulnerability of its heavy dependence on the Permian basin. Note that Targa is continuing to build further infrastructure in the basin as well. The payoff from these infrastructure requires a minimum service life as well to break-even. However, the risk remains that any future scarcity in the Permian basin area can potentially result in losses in the future. The bull-whip effect can also work in reverse and lead to significant losses and bottlenecks in future growth as well. Moreover, there is always the pressure to build the infrastructure at the right pace so that the demand for the same among O&G firms does not lag behind. However, overbuilding of infrastructure can also cause its own problems – lower utilisation, reduction in margins, congestion, reduced prices, etc.

Environmental & Regulatory Risks

One of the primary risks that Targa is facing is a common risk that most O&G companies face – environmental risk. However, along with environmental risk comes legal risk since getting environmental clearance requires legal approval. Increased methane transportation can also lead to increased pollution as well. Moreover, note that the building of infrastructure can also translate to congestion as well in the form of the traffic as well as infra congestion. As of now, the US administration has a positive stance towards the O&G industry. However, a change in government can easily change the stance as well and this comes as a risk for a midstream company like Targa Resources which requires a significant time horizon for break-even on their infrastructure.

Conclusion

So, based on the tailwind and headwind analysis of Targa Resources, the prospects look promising. Targa is a midstream company operating in a market that holds promise for the future with expected growth of 6% per year. The bullwhip effect will only amplify the growth effect. Moreover, keep in mind that Targa Resources has been actively taking steps to position itself at the helm to receive the full advantage of the external tailwinds. There are definitely risks present like environmental & regulatory risks and also the heavy dependence on the Permian basin. However, the current tailwinds are sufficient for Targa Resources to position itself as a rapidly growing midstream oil & gas company. The DCF valuation quantifies the thesis with an upside of 21%. Therefore, having considered all the factors, my recommendation for the stock would be a BUY.

data.title: Ciena Delivers Growth, But Not Value
data.description:
Thesis

Ciena Corporation (NYSE:CIEN) makes the gear that keeps the internet from falling over. Its hardware, software, and automation tools are behind the scenes, moving massive amounts of data with low lag across global networks. Telecom companies use it, hyperscalers use it, and it’s baked into the plumbing behind streaming, cloud services, AI training clusters, and 5G builds. With yesterday’s Q2 2025 earnings now in hand, my research argues that Ciena appears to be firmly aligned with the most powerful secular trends in connectivity but still lagging behind on the execution metrics needed to justify its premium valuation.

Ciena Q2 2025: Key Earnings Takeaways

Back in December 2024, I rated Ciena a “hold” after a great Q4. The company had just notched nearly 100% year-to-date returns, powered by strong fundamentals, expanding demand in cloud and AI, and its increasingly dominant position in high-performance optical tech, especially with the WaveLogic 6 Extreme rollout.

The company had momentum on nearly every front with free cash flow that was running strong, the backlog was climbing, and deal flow was coming from all the right places: telecom giants, regional carriers, and hyperscalers. At that time, the valuation had outpaced the fundamentals. The blended price-to-earnings ratio had swelled to 48x, significantly above historical norms, while earnings growth, although solid, wasn’t on a trajectory that could justify that kind of multiple without continued excellence in execution. Margins were under pressure too from inventory write-downs and a growing share of revenue tied to lower-margin cloud infrastructure deals.

As we move through mid-2025, many of those original signals remain. But they’re evolving. Ciena, essentially, is embedded in multiple secular shifts that are reshaping global communications infrastructure. The global optical networking market was worth $15.52 billion in 2024 and is expected to reach $17.35 billion this year, an 11.8% CAGR pushed by the reality that the internet is choking on its own traffic and needs to get faster, smarter, and harder to break. Industries like finance, healthcare, and digital media are pushing bandwidth requirements higher, while governments in developing markets are ramping up investments in national digital backbones.

This is all part of a bigger telecom revival. The global telecom infrastructure market, now at $211.1 billion, is on track to swell to $354.99 billion by 2035, an annual growth rate of 6.3%, slow and steady, like most things driven by bureaucracy, cables, and zoning permits. You could view this as a kind of decade-long investment, driven by a perfect storm of factors: ubiquitous mobile video consumption, 5G densification, fiber-to-the-home, and the long-overdue build-out of rural broadband. Ciena’s portfolio includes coherent optics, routing systems, and orchestration software is perfectly aligned with this upgrade cycle which gives the company a durable, embedded role in the next era of global connectivity.

Still, as most people have figured out by now, it’s the AI-everywhere push that’s pouring gas on the fire. Global data center power demand is expected to reach 130 gigawatts by 2028, growing at a 16% clip because training AI takes a lot of juice. That figure underscores the sheer scale of hyperscaler expansion: Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), along with an emerging cohort of regional GPU cluster builders and vertical AI service providers. Every one of these companies needs ultra-high throughput networking, low-latency connectivity, and automation that can scale across complex, distributed topologies.

And Ciena is meeting that moment. In this last quarter, revenue reached $1.13 billion, up 23.61% from the same time last year and $32 million ahead of what Wall Street was guessing. Meanwhile, cloud provider revenue jumped 85% year-over-year, passed $400 million, and made up 38% of the total haul.

Notably, three of Ciena’s top five customers last quarter were hyperscalers. One of them alone made up 13% of total revenue. I'll give you that that’s a concentration risk on the surface, but I think you'll also agree that it reflects Ciena’s deepening integration into the AI data center build-out. Cloud provider orders are expected to double in fiscal 2025 from the year before, driven by the scramble to build-out next-gen data center setups, including AI-focused cluster designs that eat bandwidth like candy.

Two notable contract wins also show how Ciena’s products are reaching technical and strategic needs. One of the deals involved hooking up GPU clusters with coherent optics across 100 to 150 kilometers, which matters for spreading out training loads. The other was a joint project with a big data center operator to build-out-of-band network management, the kind of back-end plumbing needed as everyone races toward self-running infrastructure.

At the product level, WaveLogic 6 Extreme is finding traction fast by adding 24 new customers in the quarter, bringing the total to 49 since its release. That’s a meaningful acceleration, especially when compared to WaveLogic 5 Extreme, which continues to grow with 10 new customers this quarter, raising its installed base to 344. Meanwhile, the WaveLogic 5 Nano pluggables, aimed at operators needing flexible, high-performance optics in compact formats, have now shipped to 178 customers. Revenue from coherent pluggables is expected to more than double year-over-year in fiscal 2025, reaching at least $150 million.

Ciena also rolled out the first 800G router with built-in 1.6T optics, a jump in both density and speed. That box helped push out a rival in India and landed eight new broadband customers in the process, an example of how stacking optics, routing, and software into one package gives Ciena leverage in competitive, margin-sensitive markets.

On the software side, Blue Planet, the company’s network automation and analytics platform, just posted its best quarter ever, with revenue just shy of $30 million. Orders for the Navigator Network Control Suite grew more than 30% in the first half of fiscal 2025. Keep in mind, telcos and cloud providers are in a rush to bolt AI onto their networks, and platforms like Blue Planet are becoming central to that effort. Its job, handling closed-loop automation, service assurance, and intent-based orchestration, makes it a kind of translator between the old network gear and whatever AI-native system comes next.

Before we move on to valuation and headwinds, financially speaking, Ciena remains in good shape. It generated $157 million in cash from operations this quarter, holds $1.35 billion in total liquidity, and returned $84 million to shareholders through stock repurchases. The company stuck to its full-year buyback target of $330 million. Management bumped up revenue growth guidance to around 14% and pointed to a growing backlog going into fiscal 2026. They admitted there are still short-term issues with product mix and supply chains, but said they’re still aiming for mid-teens operating margins by 2027.

Breaking Down Ciena’s Valuation, Growth, and Risks

Moving on to valuation, at a recent closing price of $73.05, the stock is hovering just below its modeled fair value of $73.95, a near match that might ordinarily signal market equilibrium. However, yesterday's 12.9% drop leading into that price suggests that the company's valuation by traditional measures is still undeniably elevated.

CIENA’s blended P/E ratio of 35.29x and its trailing non-GAAP P/E of 37.46x represent a premium of 67% to the sector median and over 65% above the company’s own five-year historical average.

Even its forward non-GAAP P/E stands at 32.48x, showing little valuation relief ahead. On a GAAP basis, the story is even more aggressive: a trailing P/E of 133.29x and forward P/E of 62.94x suggest a stock priced for substantial recovery and operational efficiency, none of which is yet visible in recent performance.

The key counterweight to this caution is CIENA’s growth narrative. The forward-looking PEG ratio, particularly the non-GAAP PEG at 0.93, implies that strong earnings growth could justify these high multiples.

The underlying assumption here is a 34.92% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) in earnings per share over the next three to five years. That projection is nearly three times the sector average, suggesting that CIENA is on the verge of preparing for a breakout phase, powered by that increased demand for AI-driven data transport, cloud scalability, and global 5G deployments.

The growth pitch gets a boost from Fast Graphs-style forecasts, which toss out a 36% annualized return and a future stock price north of $150 (see FAST Graphs' "Forecasting" data above). That’s more than double where the stock is now. It’s also the kind of math that makes today's bloated valuation look justifiable. The catch though is none of it shows up in the actual numbers yet.

The trailing twelve-month numbers show us that revenues declined by 7.29%, EBITDA fell by a steep 39.43%, and earnings dropped over 64% on both a GAAP and non-GAAP basis. Free cash flow got chopped in half telling us that this isn’t a company in growth mode. Still, until yesterday's reaction, the market had been squinting past all that, hoping this was just the bottom step on the way up.

Cash flow tells the same conflicted story. CIENA’s trailing price-to-cash flow is at 29.48x, with the forward number at 20.88x, both well above what’s typical for the sector. Generally, that kind of pricing assumes a big turnaround in how much cash the company can actually produce. Free cash flow per share is supposed to jump 89.48%, with operating cash flow up 52.91%. These are moonshot improvements and when they don't show up in the quarterly reports, yesterday's reaction is what's bound to happen.

Meanwhile, these recent quarterly results offer both validation and warning. CIENA beat on revenue but whiffed on earnings, putting up an adjusted EPS of $0.42, ten cents short of what Wall Street was looking for. Gross margin didn’t budge, holding at 41%, which also happened to be the lowest number in the company’s own forecast range. On the conference call, CIENA’s leadership flat-out said not to expect much on margins this quarter, and maybe a little better in the next one.

The margin problem starts with the product mix. CIENA’s fastest-growing lines, reconfigurable line systems and pluggables like the 400ZR and 800ZR, are expanding fast but bleeding margin along the way. The RLS gear runs on a razor-and-blade model, meaning the first sale is a money-loser, and profits only show up later. The pluggables are still ramping, stuck in the phase where making them costs more than it should. All in all, these products matter for CIENA's long game, especially in the cloud, but right now, they’re dragging earnings down, not lifting them up.

It's no surprise that tariffs showed up this quarter too, hitting CIENA with a mid-single-digit million-dollar charge. Management says the damage will run about $10 million per quarter and calls it “immaterial,” but it is still worth noting as another layer of complexity to the supply chain and pricing game. Workarounds are underway, but trade policy now joins the growing list of things complicating the margins.

Operating costs shot up to $369 million last quarter, overshooting CIENA’s own forecast thanks to incentive pay tied to a spike in orders. That bump locks the company into a new baseline of $360 to $370 million per quarter. Management still insists it can get gross margins back into the mid-40s and operating margins into the mid-teens by 2027, but pulling that off means everything, from product mix to execution to the macro picture, has to break their way. Meanwhile, the stock trades like the turnaround is already happening. EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT are both nearly double the industry average, even though the actual margin rebound hasn’t shown up. Revenue’s coming in, but profits are still stuck in traffic.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, CIEN remains a Hold. It’s tied to all the right long-term trends like cloud, AI, and optical networking and keeps landing big deals with hyperscalers. But the stock’s still priced like everything's already working. Valuation is high, both compared to the sector and its own history. Until margins start moving and earnings show up, it makes more sense to wait.

data.title: Stride: Dominating The K-12 Online Education Market
data.description:
Stride, Inc. (NYSE:LRN) which was formerly known as K12 Inc., is celebrating its 25th anniversary this year as a pioneer in online education. The company offers students from K-12 through college a complete digital education journey with curriculum, technology, instruction, and guidance integrated into a single platform. The stock has already risen more than 30% this year fueled by strong quarterly results and repeated financial guidance upgrades. The question now is whether this is still an attractive time to buy.

My thesis is that Stride is structurally benefiting from a shift in demand toward online education and career-focused training. Despite the recent stock price increase, I believe the valuation remains reasonable. On that basis, I give Stride a Buy rating.

Stride Has A Head Start In Digital Education

The structural growth in demand is striking. Recent polls show that more than 60% of parents are considering switching schools with 27% looking at full-time online education. This combination of dissatisfaction with traditional schools and a desire for personalized learning paths provides a solid foundation for Stride’s growth story. In addition, education policy in the US is largely in the hands of states, which increasingly offer “school choice” programs that allow parents to choose alternative education models. Stride works with dozens of charter schools and districts and seems well-positioned to benefit from this trend of decentralization.

Furthermore, Stride relies very little on federal funding, with less than 5% of its revenue coming from Washington. This makes the company relatively immune to potential federal reforms, such as the recent proposal to abolish the Department of Education. In fact, such decentralization could give Stride more room to grow in partnership with individual states.

The company offers a full school model, including curriculum, teachers, learning platform, and administrative support. While many EdTech companies focus on support or modules, Stride focuses on full educational paths including certification. Its career-focused offering stands out: high school students can choose paths in areas such as IT, healthcare, and business. They can earn certificates, collaborate with industry professionals, and even earn college credits. That is unique in the market.

Compared to competitors, Stride is doing remarkably well. Pearson, through its Connections Academy also offers online schools but has seen enrollments decline recently. Stride, on the other hand, continues to grow year after year. There are few European competitors where full-time online education at the primary or secondary school level is still rare. This makes Stride’s home market extra attractive for the time being. Other American educators such as Adtalem and Chegg focus on higher education or support services and often struggle with shrinkage or disruption. Chegg, for example, loses users to AI solutions. Stride provides full-time education which is harder for AI to replace. In addition, Stride bases its revenue on multi-year contracts with schools and districts. These provide stability and make it difficult for newcomers to quickly gain market share.

However, Stride must continue to invest in quality. The social development of students in an online environment is a point of attention. That is why the company is testing initiatives such as virtual playgrounds and organizing local meetups for students and parents. Tutoring is also being used, especially at lower school levels where (for example) reading skills are under pressure in many states. These types of investments contribute to customer satisfaction and increase retention.

Excellent Growth, Strong Balance Sheet, And Ahead of Goals

Stride’s quarterly results support the growth story. In the third quarter of fiscal 2025, revenue increased by almost 18% to $613 million. Adjusted operating profit was $141.7 million, up 47%. Net income and gross margin also grew strongly. Student enrollment increased by 21% YoY. Both the general education offering and the career-focused program are contributing to this growth.

Management expects the company to close this year with more students than at the start of the school year. This is not a given and provides a strong starting point for next year as many of those students will stay. The combination of high retention and strong enrollment is driving structural growth. Management has raised revenue guidance for FY2025 to a range of $2.370–2.385 billion with an operating income expectation of $455–465 million. This puts Stride 3 years ahead of its long-term objectives.

Financial health is good. The cash position is over $750 million against $416 million in debt. So there is no net debt. The debt that is there consists mainly of convertible bonds with an interest of 1.125% that do not mature until 2027. Stride could repay them without external financing. That gives the company flexibility for acquisitions or investments and makes it immune to interest rate increases. Free cash flow is also solid, at $37 million in Q3. There were temporarily higher accounts receivable due to the timing of government payments, but that will be compensated for in Q4.

Valuation Still Reasonable

Despite a strong rally over the past year, Stride’s valuation still leaves room for upside. At around $140 per share and expected FY2025 earnings of $7.35, the stock trades at a forward P/E of roughly 19x. That’s not cheap on an absolute basis but for a company delivering about 20% earnings growth with expanding margins and a solid balance sheet, it’s more than defensible.

Investors often overpay for growth without profitability, or they chase stable cash flows with zero growth. Stride offers both: durable, contract-backed cash flows and growth from secular tailwinds in education. That’s rare. The PEG ratio (under 1x) confirms this isn’t a hype stock; it’s a business executing in a niche that’s still in the early innings of transformation.

Compared to peers, Stride is comparable. Adtalem also trades at a forward P/E near 19x, but their revenue growth is modest and margins are thinner. Pearson doesn’t have exposure to US K-12 public contracts and is structurally less agile. And companies like Chegg are still trying to recover from the AI shockwave. Stride thrives exactly where others struggle: full-time education, funded by states, delivered at scale. That’s hard to disrupt.

Risks To Mention

And of course, there are risks. The state-level policy remains a wild card. If a major state changes its funding model or restricts online charter programs, that could temporarily affect growth. But Stride is diversified across more than 30 states with long-term contracts and deep relationships. That helps buffer against localized shocks.

Another risk is scaling quality. The company is adding thousands of students per quarter and that kind of growth can strain systems. Stride will need to keep investing in teacher support, tutoring, and engagement tools to maintain retention and outcomes. So far, they’ve shown they can do this, but it requires constant attention.

And then there’s competition. Big Tech hasn’t made a serious move into public K-12 education yet, but if Google or Amazon decides to enter the market, things could get interesting. That said, relationships with school boards, state agencies, and local districts aren’t built overnight. Stride’s first-mover advantage and regulatory expertise are difficult to replicate.

Bottom line: this isn’t a risk-free stock, but few are. What matters is that Stride’s management understands the key risks and is actively mitigating them. For long-term investors, I believe the risk/reward remains attractive. This isn’t a trade. It’s a position you build over time, in a company that’s fundamentally aligned with where the education system is headed.

Conclusion

Stride is a market leader with proven growth, a strong product, and a solid balance sheet. Demand for online and career-oriented education is increasing, state policy is favorable, and the company knows how to convert these trends into profits. Despite the strong price increase, the stock is still worth buying for long-term investors in my opinion.

My view is therefore “buy”. The valuation is fair, the risks are manageable, and the growth path is open.

There are risks. Changes in legislation could disadvantage online schools. Stride limits this risk through diversification across states and long-term contracts. Competition from new EdTech players or big tech is a potential threat, but Stride has sufficient resources to continue to innovate or to make interesting acquisitions. An economic recession or tight state budgets could slow growth in the long term, although funding per student has proven to be fairly stable so far. Operationally, Stride must continue to absorb rapid growth with sufficient qualified staff.

However, these factors are manageable and are being actively addressed by the company. Stride remains an attractive investment in a sector that is only at the beginning of a fundamental transformation.

data.title: FGM: In Good Nick This Year, But Not A Compelling Buy Now
data.description:
Enjoying Its Moment In The Sun

Given its low AUM (Assets Under Management) of only around $25m (built up over 13 years), the First Trust Germany AlphaDEX Fund (NASDAQ:FGM) certainly has the makings of a rather inconspicuous investment product. However, don’t get overly fixated on FGM’s relatively low profile, as this is a product that is now having its moment in the sun. In a year when global stocks have only risen by mid-single-digits, and developed markets have fared 3x better, FGM which offers coverage to 40 German stocks (by way of an enhanced selection methodology) has zoomed ahead by an impressive 45%!

How Is FGM Built?

FGM tracks the ‘Nasdaq AlphaDEX Germany Index’, which is an enhanced index that incorporates certain value and growth screeners during the portfolio construction process. To be more specific, the base universe here is the Nasdaq Germany Index, and stocks from this pocket are put through the wringer, by gauging a certain growth and value quotient.

On the growth front, the stocks are measured on the basis of price appreciation over 3, 6, and 12 months, the inverse sales to price ratio, and 1-year sales growth. On the value front, they are judged on the basis of return on assets (ROA), the inverse price to book value ratio, and the inverse price to cash flow ratio.

All the stocks receive an aggregate score on the basis of how they fare in the previous step, and the stocks with the top 40 scores eventually make the cut.

In the weighting process, the stocks are segregated into different quintiles, with the top-ranked quintile receiving the largest weight in the portfolio (however within each quintile, the stocks are ranked equally).

How Does FGM Compare To The Largest German-themed ETF—EWG?

On paper, FGM has a rather intriguing set-up, but how does it actually stack up against the big daddy of German-based ETFs? The ETF in question is the iShares MSCI Germany ETF (EWG) which has been around for close to three decades, and accumulated an AUM of over $2800m, which is 114x the AUM of FGM!

It appears that big is better, at least from a total return perspective, as FGM has underperformed EWG by around 2600 bps since the former made its debut in 2012.

EWG’s superiority isn’t only felt at the total return level; even at the risk-adjusted return level (be it over the short term or the long term), the larger product has dazzled. For instance, take a look at the respective Sharpe ratios, which highlight the outperformance over the risk-free rate for every unit of risk taken.

The Sortino ratio is a moderation of the Sharpe ratio, in that it only looks at excess return capabilities in light of the downside deviation experienced by both these products. Yet again, the honors go to EWG.

So not only has FGM underperformed EWG, it has also not been too great at juggling its risk profile.

Also note that FGM is less cost-efficient than EWG, with an expense ratio that is 30bps higher. FGM’s enhanced methodology also means that this is a portfolio susceptible to a great deal of upheaval. One can get a sense of this through the annual turnover ratio, which suggests that pretty much all holdings get turned over a year. In contrast, EWG is incredibly stable, with a portfolio churn of just 3%. Because FGM doesn’t see a great deal of interest from tactical players (it witnesses daily dollar volumes that are not even 0.5% of the volumes that EWG witnesses on averages), the spread between the bid and the ask is quite wide (an average rate of 0.37%). This is not something that EWG suffers from.

Hitherto, all that we’ve written in this section would imply that FGM is a total washout, but that isn’t the case. Firstly, its screening mechanism is such that the top 10 holdings don’t take up as strong a weight as EWG’s holdings. This is commendable because EWG covers a much wider pool of names. EWG is also heavily tilted towards giant-caps, whereas FGM tilts more towards stocks that are a level lower, in addition to offering quite a useful chunk of mid-cap stocks (27%, as opposed to just 5% for EWG). What this means is that investors are likely to gain access to stocks with a superior earnings growth runway, as mid-caps are still further back in the overall lifecycle (as opposed to giant-caps that are closer to hitting a saturation point). Note that FGM’s holdings are poised to deliver double-digit earnings growth, which is almost 200bps better than what EWG’s holdings are poised to deliver. Crucially, despite covering a higher growth component, FGM’s valuations are far more compelling than EWG’s (a P/E differential of 26% in favor of FGM).

On the income front, both products don’t offer any dividends as of now, but note that historically FGM has followed a quarterly payout system (EWG follows a semi-annual calendar), which enables investors to reinvest dividends more frequently.

Is FGM ETF A Good Buy Now?

If FGM is to carry on its good run, it will need the support of the industrials side of this portfolio, as those stocks account for the largest stake at 38%. It looks like things are convalescing well in the manufacturing sector with PMIs over the last three months, hovering around the 48 mark, much higher than what has been seen over the preceding 12 months. However, it’s also important to note that these are still contractionary readings (anything below 50).

Germany industrials are clearly not at full tilt, as utilization levels are currently only around the 77% mark (a lot lower than the 45-year average of 83%), although investors will be encouraged to note that export expectations have spiked up in May, particularly with regard to mechanical engineering, automotive and electrical products. Needless to say, things could change quite quickly, if the EU and the US are unable to arrive at an acceptable trade solution by the 9th of July.

Because of ambiguities associated with the trade landscape, we feel that jumping into FGM now would represent something of a risky proposition, particularly as the ETF has already rallied a great deal over the past two months or so, and has now almost approached potential resistance. Meanwhile, note that on the weekly chart, the RSI is signalling overbought conditions.

Another reason not to get too hung about FGM now is because, its previously oversold relative strength ratio (versus the broader European markets), has pretty much mean-reverted to its long-term average.

data.title: Nvidia: I Predicted $150 By Year End - I Was Dead Wrong
data.description:
In my previous coverage of Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), I predicted the stock would test the $150 price level by year-end.

I was dead wrong.

At this pace, I believe the stock will reach my price target in the next month or two.

With the recent earnings season, it's time to revisit my bull case. I advance in this intro that my biggest concern of a growth slowdown in the data center compute segment due to tariffs is now gone after Nvidia reported a 76% YOY revenue increase in the past quarter in this segment.

Furthermore, management reaffirmed their gross margin target of mid-70s% by year-end. I believe one of the drivers is the drop in lower margin sales to China, following the new export controls that limit sales of the H20 AI chip in this region.

As you are about to find out, the biggest risk to my bull case is the fact that The Street is starting to take this level of outperformance for granted. If Nvidia fails to hit the market with the B30 (an upcoming export-compliant replacement of the H20), and considering the $8 billion headwind expected in FQ2 2025, I believe the company is at risk of missing the top-line consensus. As for when this risk might materialize, I honestly don’t know, and I don’t think anyone else does either. But it’s a risk worth keeping in mind.

Is this risk enough to grant this stock anything less than a strong buy? I believe the answer is no. Below, I break down the reasons why.

Data Center Growth Intact

Given the kerfuffle over reciprocal and sectoral tariffs that led to a 20% drop in the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX), tanking almost every growth stock in the U.S., I was curious to see if the demand for AI had shown any signs of weakness.

In my view, one of the best ways to check that is by looking at Nvidia’s financial statements.

Total data center revenue was up by 73% YOY. Below is a chart showing the trend for the past few years.

To put Nvidia's data center revenue into context, I thought about adding a chart below that compares it with Advanced Micro Devices' (AMD) and Intel’s (INTC) data center revenue.

The difference is abysmal, to say the least.

Traditionally, Nvidia's CFOs have been kind enough to break down the data center segment by splitting the contributions of compute (i.e., GPUs like the Blackwell and CPUs like the Grace) and networking (i.e., network switches, like the Quantum-X800 InfiniBand).

Well, it turns out that data center compute was up by 76% YOY in the last quarter, surpassing the overall data center growth. If this is not a sign that demand for AI is intact, I don't know what is.

To me, the data center compute segment is the best way to really understand what's going on with the demand for AI. Why is that?

Data center infrastructure companies, like Nebius (NBIS) or CoreWeave (CRWV), along with hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Amazon's (AMZN) AWS, need Nvidia's AI accelerators to satisfy the insane computing demand for their own (or their customers) large language models, or LLMs. Why did I use the word insane?

Well, here is a fun fact from CFO Colette Kress during the latest earnings conference call (emphasis added):

On average, major hyperscalers are each deploying nearly 1,000 NVL72 racks or 72,000 Blackwell GPUs per week and are on track to further ramp output this quarter.

To put this insane demand for computing power into perspective, Azure OpenAI processed more than 100 trillion tokens in the last quarter. That's a 5x increase from the same period last year.

Unsurprisingly, the rapid adoption of the Blackwell architecture, particularly the GB200 AI chip, comprised of 36 Grace CPUs + 72 Blackwell GPU, used in the GB200 NVL72 rack (composed of 36 GB200 AI chips) is driving the high double-digit growth rates in the data center segment.

According to CFO Colette, most of the revenue in the data center compute segment comes from the Blackwell series (emphasis added):

Blackwell contributed nearly 70% of Data Center compute revenue in the quarter with a transition from Hopper nearly complete.

The new(ish) GPU family surely means higher margins for the company, right? Yes and no. More details on the next section.

The Decline In Gross Margins

I have a close friend who buys stocks that make sense (like me, he is also a sub blue-collar practitioner). But unlike me, he never checks the news or reads conference call transcripts.

If you’re not familiar with Nvidia’s story, the trend in its gross margin might really catch you off guard. Here’s a chart that shows what I mean.

Yikes! 60% gross margin in the past quarter, the lowest since Q1 2023 (emphasis, 2023 not 2024). We're talking about a 1300 bps sequential decline. What happened?

Well, the US administration is curbing China’s access to AI accelerators from Nvidia, AMD, and Intel. In the infamous April 15 8-K filing, Nvidia informed its shareholders that the export controls to China (including Hong Kong, Macau, and D:5 countries) are expected to lead to $5.5 billion in inventory charges associated with the H20 GPUs.

Well, it turns out that the headwind was lower than expected. Nvidia reported $4.5 billion in inventory charges in FQ1 2026, as they reused certain materials from the H20s. Naturally, this write-down led to an increase in COGS, which tanked gross margins.

In the earnings call, the CFO stated that, excluding this charge, the FQ1 non GAAP gross margin would have been 71% (in line with the expectation in FQ4 2025). Therefore, I'm not sweating a drop about the apparent decline in gross margins, as Nvidia's core GPU (i.e., the Blackwell series) is still driving spectacular margins.

From a revenue perspective, Nvidia expects a decline of $8 billion in FQ2 2026 due to the export control rules related to the H20. Even with this headwind, the CFO projected $45 billion in revenue, plus or minus 2%. That's a 50% YOY increase and approximately $1 billion more than in the past quarter. Not bad considering that China accounted for 12.5% of last quarter's revenue. From a margins perspective, the company is guiding a non-GAAP gross margin of 72% (driven by the higher margin sales of the Blackwell series). Furthermore, management has reaffirmed their mid-70% non-GAAP gross margin target for the end of this fiscal year. In other words, less (lower margin) sales in China mean higher margins for the company.

As a final note, Nvidia hasn't completely ditched the China region. According to recent reports, the company is currently working on the B30 AI chip, which is designed to meet the current export control restrictions. I recommend keeping a close eye on the development of this new AI chip, as it could reignite sales in this region.

Price Action

To my eyes, the chart below looks great. The stock is on track to test all-time highs and, considering the strong narrative and growth, I believe it will break the $150 support over the next few months. I just don't see anything material prior to FQ2 2026 earnings that could stop this momentum. That said, remember the following quote from Taleb: absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.

Finally, the RSI is at a healthy level, despite the rally since early April.

Institutional Activity

A quick look at the 13F data for Q1 2025 shows what one would initially expect: a slight decline in institutional ownership and new positions in this stock. Please bear in mind that this data is compiled until the end of March. With "liberation day" just a few days later, I am not surprised to see funds not buying this stock in Q1.

With Q2 coming soon to an end, I recommend keeping a close eye on institutional activity once the 13Fs are fully complied at the end of the 45-day deadline (i.e., mid-July). In my view, it would be highly favorable to see a double-digit increase in new positions, along with an increase in the number of funds holding this stock.

Risks

The problem with growing at a fast pace is that the Street starts to expect it. Check out the chart below to see how often revenue has beaten analyst forecasts (and by how much).

The top-line surprise in the past quarter was the lowest since FQ4 2023. Therefore, the biggest risk that I see right now is a miss due to a slower than expected quarter. I have no doubt that would trigger a high single digit selloff.

Other risks include the Trump administration banning the B30 AI chips. As I said, Nvidia is still in the design phase, according to some reports. If the company fails to hit the market with the B30s, I believe the risk of missing the analyst consensus for the top line is quite real.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating. The recent earnings report shows that the growth in the data center compute segment is intact, despite the tariff drama earlier this year. Speaking of dramas, the new export controls related to the H20 AI chips have led to a considerable decline in gross margins. Did the market care about this? I believe the answer is no, as gross margins when excluding this headwind, were in line with the company's FQ4 2025 guidance.

With regard to guidance, management expects revenue to grow by 50% YOY in the next quarter, despite the $8 billion headwind in sales due to the ban of the H20 sales in China. It seems that the company is already working in a fully compliant new generation of AI chips, the B30, which could bring back some of the revenue loss in China.

Overall, I see the narrative intact, the growth impressive, and even though the Street is pricing in this outperformance, I believe there is still an upside ahead. I foresee a short life for the $150 support level.

data.title: Tesla Is Trading Solely On Elon, Robotaxis Appear Set To Fail
data.description:
Elon Musk Is the Valuation

If you are here fully convinced Tesla, Inc.’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) valuation is so high because of how progressive and great this company is, you have experienced yourself a “Musk Premium.” In this article, I won’t be pretending Tesla trades on fundamentals because well, it doesn’t, and it never really has. It trades on CEO Elon Musk, on his, without a doubt, brilliant vision and beautiful stories about the future, and for years, it worked. Up until a few months ago, Musk was seen as a visionary and a genius, and Tesla's stock price reflected just that. But does this era come to an end just before our eyes?

Since Elon Musk began his “political career,” his character in the eyes of the public started to shift from an idol he once was to the devil, who has no idea what he is doing. Probably all of you remember that in early 2025, Musk was appointed head of the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). It was supposed to be the department that saved the government some money, but instead, it turned into chaos. Musk, as the head of DOGE, tried to push for controversial policies like eliminating USAID and the Department of Education, and caused almost 300,000 U.S. citizens to lose their jobs, mostly in governmental institutions. After only 130 days, he stepped down.

Elon Musk's political efforts caused Tesla stock to pull back in price significantly. Not surprisingly, at least for me, this time not only was the stock hurt, but also Tesla in the field, where people are supposed to buy cars. As a result, just a few months later, we can see that deliveries and revenues are dropping, while competitors, like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), are confidently taking Tesla’s lost market share to themselves. Of course, Elon Musk still has the power to boost the stock price with buzzwords and promises, like robotaxis, AGI, Optimus, etc.

But with the latest Elon Musk-Donald Trump public pressure, if I were a Tesla investor, I would be seriously concerned. Tesla’s valuation is inextricably tied to Elon Musk, and if Elon Musk seriously annoys President Donald Trump, who is not the most easy-going person when it comes to ego-games and happens to be the most powerful man today in the U.S., the company could be hit immensely.

Right now, it looks like Musk is open to making peace (not that this is surprising, in this battle, I don’t think he stood a high chance), and Trump says everything’s “going very well.” There’s even a phone call scheduled. I am curious how this story goes because it’s one of the factors that could continue to hurt Tesla’s stock price, but fundamentally, it doesn’t change anything. And now I will explain why.

Valuation Metrics: The 900% Illusion

The stock appears overvalued immensely based on almost all of the metrics, like P/E, P/S, EV/EBITDA, etc. This is the Musk premium I mentioned at the beginning. Without a doubt, the company was one of a kind in 2019, and I get why people were so excited back then. Elon Musk is a brilliant man, and his vision and ideas have helped humanity make huge progress. But now, it’s starting to look like the edge we saw changed with regard to Elon Musk, and his focus has shifted from innovation. Besides, Tesla is not the same as it was five years ago. It appears there are many companies that can build the same products, some are cheaper, some are of better quality. So if the battle with the President doesn’t end well for Elon Musk, and there are several ways how Trump could hurt him, what will be left of Tesla?

Let’s break this down: Tesla’s forward P/E ratio is over 180, compared to a sector median of around 16. EV/EBITDA? Tesla is trading at 76x, versus an industry average closer to 9x. EV/Sales? Tesla sits at 11x, while traditional automakers trade closer to 1x. Hopefully, you can see for yourself that this is not just expensive, it’s disconnected from reality.

If you take Elon Musk out of the equation and the brand that many people still truly believe in, I believe this stock would trade at $20–25, and the company definitely wouldn’t be a trillion-dollar empire, it just was before the news hit the stock price. I am not saying this is a fair value for the stock price because, let’s be real, the brand is one of the strongest on the market, even after this reputational hit. But still, as far as Musk's bad reputation goes, the more Tesla's stock price will incline toward reality. It has a long way to go because the reality is that people do not want to buy Teslas after Musk's publicity in the office. And when I say people, I don't even mean in the U.S. My friends in Israel, where I live, in Ukraine, where I am from, and all over Europe, are selling their Teslas to not associate with this person.

Market Share Collapse: From Dominance to Decline

I am following Tesla’s news, and I read sometimes what my colleagues on Seeking Alpha have to say about this company, and this stock gets so much coverage that I don’t feel like I have a lot to add besides my own vision, perspective, and observations. In Israel, on roads in the past year, from a majority of electric vehicles being Teslas and a few Chinese brands here and there, we switched to a huge number of BYDs, with some other Chinese brands like XPeng (XPEV), and Tesla seriously losing this battle.

I actually can say that the area I live in is a higher-middle class area with mostly young families that are working in tech companies, there is a similar number of BYDs to some very popular brands here, like Mazda or Toyota. I also noticed that many tech companies give out BYD to their workers in leasing or as a work vehicle. I didn’t share this observation of mine before I saw Tesla sales in the UK and Europe because I thought it was some trend only here, in central Israel. But now we know that Tesla's sales were down 36% in the UK in May 2025 alone. Meanwhile, BYD outsold Tesla there.

But it’s not the only problem. Margins are also under pressure. Tesla as a company has the support of higher-margin segments like energy and storage, but still, their overall profitability is weakening. To get to the real margin, I looked at just the automotive sales revenue, which was $12.9 billion, not including leasing or anything else. Then I subtracted the cost of automotive sales, which is approximately $11.5 billion. That leaves a gross profit of about $1.46 billion and an automotive margin of 11.3%. By the way, if you take out $595 million in regulatory credits, you will get the margin closer to 8–9%. All of the numbers are from the operations statement from the Q1 presentation:

For context, two years ago, Tesla’s vehicle margin was about 25%. Calculate for yourself the drop in margins, but I got that it’s more than 50%. And yet, the stock still trades like margins are expanding and everything is fine. AI-powered Google search provided the information about the margins back in 2022 and 2023:

Besides, let’s not forget that in Q1 2025, Tesla’s automotive revenue dropped 20% year-over-year. Earnings per share fell by over 50%. Profit margins are the lowest they've been since 2019. And despite that, the stock trades at more than 10x sales, over 180x forward earnings, and nearly 77x forward EBITDA.

Robotaxi Fantasy vs Reality

I often hear that bulls refer to robotaxis as something that will change the company and bring prosperity. But will it? I don’t know if you saw the information that Alphabet's (GOOG) Waymo taxis are equipped much better than Tesla and that Tesla most likely won’t be able to launch robotaxis, at least not the ones that won’t be controlled by humans. Yes, yes, the only robotaxi possibility we are facing right now, it’s not even a robotaxi. Waymo is powered by the fifth-generation Waymo Driver and guided by five lidar sensors, six radars, and 29 cameras. Tesla simply upgraded its software and called it Robotaxi. It has only eight cameras.

Besides, Tesla's Full Self-Driving ("FSD") is Level 2, which means it requires constant driver supervision. And you have Waymo on the other hand, which has been at Level 4 for a long time already, and they are already working, people are using them and showing real results. Tesla’s promise of full autonomy has been delayed for years, and yet, the company hasn’t even reached Level 3 autonomy, which allows limited hands-off operation. Even if Tesla somehow reaches Level 3 soon and then immediately Level 4, what so big will happen? It’s not the first mover anymore in this space. I genuinely don’t understand why all of the buzz.

Also, Tesla quietly replaced the $25K Model 2 with a robotaxi promise. So instead of building something more affordable, which was actually also promised before, they went all in on something that doesn’t even have a timeline, let alone regulatory approval. This is another confirmation that the stock isn’t driven by deliveries, but by stories and narratives.

What's Tesla Worth Without Musk?

So, let’s ask the obvious: what is Tesla worth without the Musk premium? Based on current valuation metrics, Tesla is trading at about 900% above the sector median. That would imply a fair value of around $20–25 per share if it were some other average, not-so-hyped stock. Besides Musk, I don’t see why the company is priced so high, and of course, the stock can stay so expensive for a long time from now, because people like to fall in love with hyped stories and put their money in them. But I know for a fact that when this Musk bubble bursts, I won’t be the one to lose my money on it.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Thornburg Income & U.S. Government Bonds Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The first quarter of 2025 was characterized by significant volatility and complexity across global financial markets that was not driven by a singular trend but was a tug-of-war between multiple forces. Aggressive fiscal measures, particularly new tariffs, injected uncertainty and inflationary pressures, initially steepening the yield curve.

However, the Federal Reserve maintained a patient stance, signaling potential rate cuts later in the year amid concerns of slowing economic momentum. This dovish tilt capped longer-term yields and supported risk assets, including corporate credit, which saw modest spread tightening despite the macro headwinds. Inflation-linked bonds also garnered attention as tariff-induced price pressures became a focal point. The quarter concluded with markets closely watching inflation data and central bank rhetoric for clearer signals on the future path of rates and the ultimate impact of fiscal policy on the broader economy.

First Quarter 2025 Performance Highlights

  • In 1Q25, the Limited Term Income Portfolio (I share class) returned 2.27%, -15 basis points behind the Bloomberg Intermediate U.S. Government/Credit Index. Year to date, the portfolio (I share class) returned 2.27%, -15 basis points behind the index.
  • The portfolio maintained a duration roughly 0.28 years shorter than the index, which was problematic during a period of falling yields. As a result, duration was a detractor from relative performance, accounting for -11bps during the quarter.
  • The portfolio’s performance during the quarter was driven by its underweight allocation to Treasuries securities versus the Index which accounted for -65bps of relative underperformance.
  • The portfolio’s relative performance did benefit from its larger allocation to Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMO) and Mortgage Pass-Through (MPT) securities. The allocation to CMOs contributed 25bps to relative performance during the quarter while the MPT exposure contributed 26bps to relative performance.

Current Positioning and Outlook

Consistent with our mandate, we actively adjusted portfolio positioning throughout the quarter in response to evolving market conditions and relative value opportunities. We maintained broad diversification across fixed-income sectors. While detailed shifts are part of our continuous management, our active management approach proved crucial in navigating the quarter's volatility.

The strategy remained positioned with a longer duration and higher quality bias compared to its long-term range, driven by attractive real yields and very tight credit spreads. However, the market rewarded the opposite. Security selection, driven by our bottom-up fundamental credit research, was a key contributor, allowing us to identify resilient issuers across various sectors.

We maintained broad diversification across fixed-income sectors with an emphasis on sectors demonstrating resilience or offering compelling risk/reward profiles identified through our research. The allocations to Treasuries, investment grade corporate credit, collateralized mortgage obligations, and mortgage pass-throughs were all large drivers of absolute returns during the period.

The portfolio continued to invest across the investment-grade credit spectrum, focused on fundamentally sound issuers offering attractive compensation for risk. This included selectively chosen corporate bonds with stronger balance sheets and more predictable cash flows which helped drive absolute performance during the quarter. Our primary approach remained identifying mispriced securities through rigorous bottom-up analysis, but opportunities were few and far between given spread levels.

Looking ahead, uncertainty surrounding inflation, central bank policy paths, economic growth trajectories, and geopolitical events, including trade policy, persists. We anticipate continued market volatility, which often creates opportunities for active managers. We remain committed to our disciplined investment process, combining top-down macroeconomic insights with intensive bottom-up credit research to identify relative value across the fixed income universe. Our focus continues to be on generating attractive income and risk-adjusted total returns for our clients over the long term.

data.title: REITs: Income Goldmine Or Value Pitfall?
data.description:
REITs are one of the fundamental cornerstones for most income-based strategies and portfolios. They embody the necessary characteristics that support durable current income, inflation protection and gradual price appreciation.

I like to label REITs as quasi-equity instruments. With a quasi-equity I mean a combination of bond-like and equity-like features.

The bond element comes through the following items:

  • Long-term lease agreements with relatively fixed prices (similar to coupons).
  • Relatively minor organic growth opportunities due to the requirement to distribute the lion's share of earnings in dividends.
  • Generally, protected asset base with quite low business risk.

The equity element kicks in through the following items:

  • Leases tied to annual fixed rate or CPI-linked escalators.
  • The ability to secure some growth by reinvesting the undistributed cash amount, or cash that is collected by selling properties at a higher price than what was initially invested.
  • The ability to issue fresh equity that could be combined with an additional leverage to expand the portfolio in an accretive fashion.

The bond factors render the valuations relatively low, which, in turn, help drive up the dividend yields.

The equity factors bring in the dividend inflation protection aspect as well as the potential for a gradual price appreciation return component.

Again, if we put these things together, I think you will agree with me that we arrive at an investment product that fits very well into most income based strategies/portfolios.

Having said that, there is a catch.

And the catch is that nobody wants to have high exposure to an asset base that systematically underperforms other income alternatives. And the situation becomes even worse if the dividends themselves do not match or exceed CPI (inflation) levels in the economy on the ground.

Since 2022, REITs could be put into that category.

Namely, since interest rates started to go up, the REITs followed, but just in the opposite direction.

Now, if we turn back to the quasi-equity story and zoom into the bond-like features, we will have to conclude that all of this makes sense. Bonds are high duration assets that are negatively correlated to the interest rates. The higher the yields, the lower the bond prices. The same applies to REITs. For some more, for some less, but, in general, it is relevant for the lion's share of the REIT players - hence, the drop in the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VNQ).

The question now is what the direction of travel will be going forward.

Could it be so that REITs will continue to remain depressed and underperform income-producing alternatives such as MLPs, dividend blue-chip corporates and various CEFs? Or is this actually an opportunity to invest heavily before the recovery takes place?

Let me discuss this in the next section.

REITs: Trap or opportunity?

I know that some of you will not be happy about this answer, but my response is that we really do not know. The reason for this is that it all depends on the systematic factors that determine the pathway for long-term yields.

Having said that, I think that it is possible though to understand which scenario is more likely than the other (i.e., REITs going up or REITs continuing to underperform).

In my humble opinion, the pessimistic scenario is more likely.

I give you three reasons for this.

First, even though REITs have experienced a decent contraction in their valuation multiples, which, theoretically, should imply an interesting opportunity, in reality, the entry point is not that attractive.

What really drives REIT valuations are the levels and its changes in the long-term yield space. This is why, for example, the recent base rate cuts have not moved the needle for REIT valuations. And if we compare REIT yields with the long-term "risk-free" yields, then the situation looks rather bad. The spread is negative. To get the spread back to the historical norm, one of the following things has to happen:

  • Long-term yields going down.
  • REIT dividend yields going up.

Second, and this is where I think the rubber hits the road. In my view, the likelihood of the long-term yields going down any time soon is extremely low. Let's take a look at what happens with the fiscal deficits, geopolitics, foreign capital outflows from the USD assets etc. These are items that are inherently supportive for having high and even rising long-term yields. So, the only realistic option that remains is REITs getting repriced lower to warrant a healthy and positive spread from the U.S. Treasury yields.

Third, we should not rule out an increasing inflation rate. The presence of notable tariffs, de-globalization, production reshoring from low-cost regions etc., are all inflationary items. Granted, so far, we have not seen material effects from the newly introduced tariffs, but we should definitely be cognizant of lagged-effects. In case the inflation level goes up, the chances are quite high that the base rates will not get reduced to, say, ~2% level. I know that a long-term yield level is what matters for REITs, but having a friendly base rate at least allows saving on interest costs through credit facilities and shorter term debt instruments. Plus, higher inflation will then make the real (not nominal) yield spread between REIT yields and the U.S. Treasury yield even less attractive for investors.

Investor action items

In a nutshell, I do not see a sector-wide bullish case for REITs any time soon.

However, it does not mean that there are no opportunities at all in this space.

Given the macro picture that I outlined above, I see the following investment plays that REIT investors could explore:

1. Real estate closed-end fund instruments: In case the base rates fall and the inflation risk does not materialize (or at least to such an extent that would keep the Fed away from cutting the rates), real estate CEF funds could deliver very strong returns. Since CEF vehicles tend to assume leverage through short-term debt instruments to magnify their overall portfolio yields, then the situation in which the base rates fall, but the asset yields remain stable or even rise could lead to an enhanced spread capture. Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (NYSE:RNP) is a great example with short-term debt focused leverage and exposure to relatively high-yielding and solid quality REITs.

2. Competitive cost of debt REITs: At its core, the risk of high long-term yields is about unfavorable cost of debt. It is clear that there are no mechanisms for REITs to mitigate the long-term yield risk against which their bonds/borrowings are priced. Yet, what they can do is to keep the credit risk premiums as low as possible. The way to do that is through fortress balance sheets and upper investment grade credit ratings. In this case, Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O) is a REIT to consider (i.e., one of the few REITs with upper investment grade balance sheets), especially given how low the multiple has become, at ~P/FFO 13.1x, while the 10-year historical average is ~17.0x.

3. Screaming bargains: One can never go wrong with picking up securities that trade way below their intrinsic values. For example, in my view, this is the case for Alexandria Real Estate Equities Inc. (NYSE:ARE), which also has an upper investment grade balance sheet, ~60% FFO payout, resilient portfolio and positive lease spread statistics. However, largely due to the market fears of pharma and biotech companies reducing their R&D spend levels, ARE's stock has been completely hammered. The P/FFO has dropped to 7.6x and the dividend has jumped to 7.5% (well over the U.S. Treasury yield). Please see more details on this case in my recent ARE-specific article: Alexandria Real Estate: The True Definition Of A Dividend Bargain.
